module_title: Episode 3: Chocolate — Food of the Gods
title: Knowledge Check: Chocolate
text_content:
This check pulls from all four lenses in Episode 3: geography, social studies, economics, and language.

Each time you take it, you get 10 questions drawn from a larger pool, and the answer choices are shuffled, so a retake is never the same test twice. You need 80 percent to pass, and you can retake it as many times as you like. The goal is understanding, not gatekeeping.

After each question you see an explanation and a citation. Read them. The reasons a wrong answer is wrong are the best study material here.

When you finish, take the Project assignment for this episode, where the bigger questions, the journey from food of the gods to candy bar and the gap between what chocolate costs and what farmers earn, get the long-form treatment they deserve.
quiz_content: {"passingScore":80,"attemptsAllowed":-1,"questionsPerAttempt":10,"shuffleOptions":true,"questions":[{"id":"q1","questionText":"What does the scientific name Theobroma cacao mean?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q1a","text":"Food of the gods"},{"id":"q1b","text":"Bitter bean"},{"id":"q1c","text":"Sweet tree"},{"id":"q1d","text":"Forest fruit"}],"correctOptionId":"q1a","explanation":"Theobroma is Greek for food of the gods, a fitting name for a plant the Maya and Aztec treated as sacred. It is not a reference to bitterness, sweetness, or fruit.","citation":"Lesson: Chocolate, the Food of the Gods"},{"id":"q2","questionText":"How wide is the cacao growing belt compared with other commodities?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q2a","text":"The narrowest of any major commodity, about 20 degrees North to 20 degrees South"},{"id":"q2b","text":"The widest, from 42 North to 33 South"},{"id":"q2c","text":"The same as coffee"},{"id":"q2d","text":"It grows at any latitude"}],"correctOptionId":"q2a","explanation":"Cacao grows only between about 20 degrees North and 20 degrees South, the narrowest belt of any major commodity. Tea has the widest range, and coffee runs to 25 and 30 degrees.","citation":"Lesson: The Cacao Tree and the Narrowest Belt"},{"id":"q3","questionText":"Cacao is an understory tree. What does that mean?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q3a","text":"It grows in shade beneath a taller forest canopy, not in open sun"},{"id":"q3b","text":"It grows taller than every other tree"},{"id":"q3c","text":"It grows underground"},{"id":"q3d","text":"It grows only in deserts"}],"correctOptionId":"q3a","explanation":"An understory tree grows beneath a taller canopy, in shade. Cacao wants 50 to 70 percent shade, which is why it is often grown under a managed forest canopy.","citation":"Lesson: The Cacao Tree and the Narrowest Belt"},{"id":"q4","questionText":"What pollinates cacao flowers, and why does it tie cacao to the forest?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q4a","text":"Tiny midges that live in forest-floor litter, so cacao needs forest biodiversity to fruit"},{"id":"q4b","text":"Honeybees, which can live anywhere"},{"id":"q4c","text":"Wind, so forest is not needed"},{"id":"q4d","text":"Hummingbirds"}],"correctOptionId":"q4a","explanation":"Cacao is pollinated mainly by tiny midges that breed in the damp litter of a real forest floor. No forest means fewer midges and less fruit, so cacao depends on the forest to reproduce.","citation":"Lesson: The Cacao Tree and the Narrowest Belt"},{"id":"q5","questionText":"Which country grows the most cacao, about 42 percent of the world's supply?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q5a","text":"Cote d'Ivoire"},{"id":"q5b","text":"Ghana"},{"id":"q5c","text":"Mexico"},{"id":"q5d","text":"Ecuador"}],"correctOptionId":"q5a","explanation":"Cote d'Ivoire grows about 42 percent of the world's cacao, the largest producer. Ghana is second at about 17 percent. Mexico is cacao's homeland but a small producer today.","citation":"Lesson: The Big Producers and the Cacao Varieties"},{"id":"q6","questionText":"What is the geographic irony about where cacao grows today?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q6a","text":"Cacao is from the Americas, but most of it now grows in West Africa"},{"id":"q6b","text":"Cacao is from Africa, but most now grows in Asia"},{"id":"q6c","text":"Cacao only grows where it was first domesticated"},{"id":"q6d","text":"Cacao now grows mainly in Europe"}],"correctOptionId":"q6a","explanation":"Cacao was domesticated and made sacred in Mesoamerica, yet today Cote d'Ivoire and Ghana, in West Africa, grow well over half of it. Belgium and Switzerland grow none.","citation":"Lesson: The Big Producers and the Cacao Varieties"},{"id":"q7","questionText":"Which cacao variety makes up about 80 percent of world production?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q7a","text":"Forastero, a hardy, basic, somewhat bitter cacao"},{"id":"q7b","text":"Criollo, a rare and complex cacao"},{"id":"q7c","text":"Nacional, an heirloom cacao"},{"id":"q7d","text":"Trinitario, a balanced hybrid"}],"correctOptionId":"q7a","explanation":"About 80 percent of production is hardy, basic Forastero. Criollo is rare (about 2 percent) and can sell for many times more, while Trinitario is a smaller hybrid share.","citation":"Lesson: The Big Producers and the Cacao Varieties"},{"id":"q8","questionText":"About how much does a cacao farmer in Cote d'Ivoire often earn per day?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q8a","text":"About 0.50 to 0.84 dollars"},{"id":"q8b","text":"About 50 dollars"},{"id":"q8c","text":"About 6 dollars"},{"id":"q8d","text":"About 100 dollars"}],"correctOptionId":"q8a","explanation":"Cacao farmers in Cote d'Ivoire often earn only about 0.50 to 0.84 dollars a day, far below the roughly 6 dollars a day needed to live. That gap drives the episode's economics.","citation":"Lesson: The Big Producers and the Cacao Varieties"},{"id":"q9","questionText":"What could a 2 degree Celsius temperature rise do to cacao-growing land?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q9a","text":"Make up to 89 percent of today's suitable land unsuitable"},{"id":"q9b","text":"Double the suitable land"},{"id":"q9c","text":"Have no measurable effect"},{"id":"q9d","text":"Move cacao to the poles"}],"correctOptionId":"q9a","explanation":"A 2 degree rise could make up to 89 percent of currently suitable cacao land unsuitable, because cacao depends on a fragile lowland forest and cannot move to cooler high ground.","citation":"Lesson: A Two-Degree Threat"},{"id":"q10","questionText":"Why is cacao more exposed to warming than coffee?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q10a","text":"It grows at low elevation, so it cannot climb to cooler high ground"},{"id":"q10b","text":"It grows only on mountaintops"},{"id":"q10c","text":"It needs frost to survive"},{"id":"q10d","text":"It does not need water"}],"correctOptionId":"q10a","explanation":"Cacao grows in lowland forest, so unlike coffee it cannot escape heat by moving uphill. It also depends on shade and forest midges, which warming disrupts.","citation":"Lesson: A Two-Degree Threat"},{"id":"q11","questionText":"What is agroforestry, the main defense for cacao against a warming climate?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q11a","text":"Growing cacao under a managed forest canopy instead of clearing the land"},{"id":"q11b","text":"Clearing all forest for full sun"},{"id":"q11c","text":"Growing cacao indoors"},{"id":"q11d","text":"Moving cacao to the desert"}],"correctOptionId":"q11a","explanation":"Agroforestry grows cacao under shade trees, which cool the soil, hold moisture, and keep the pollinators alive. Clearing the forest removes the shade and midges cacao depends on.","citation":"Lesson: A Two-Degree Threat"},{"id":"q12","questionText":"Which people are credited with the earliest known cacao use, around 3500 BCE?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q12a","text":"The Olmec"},{"id":"q12b","text":"The Aztec"},{"id":"q12c","text":"The Spanish"},{"id":"q12d","text":"The British"}],"correctOptionId":"q12a","explanation":"The Olmec began harvesting wild cacao around 3500 BCE, the earliest known use. The Aztec came much later, and the Spanish arrived only in the 1500s.","citation":"Lesson: Food of the Gods"},{"id":"q13","questionText":"In the Maya Popol Vuh creation story, humanity is finally made from what?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q13a","text":"Corn and cacao"},{"id":"q13b","text":"Gold and silver"},{"id":"q13c","text":"Clay only"},{"id":"q13d","text":"Sugar and milk"}],"correctOptionId":"q13a","explanation":"In the Popol Vuh, after failed attempts from mud and then wood, humanity is finally made from corn and cacao, placing cacao at the center of creation.","citation":"Lesson: Food of the Gods"},{"id":"q14","questionText":"In the Aztec world, cacao beans were used as what?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q14a","text":"Money; for example, a turkey cost about 100 beans"},{"id":"q14b","text":"Only as seeds for planting"},{"id":"q14c","text":"A building material"},{"id":"q14d","text":"A type of jewelry only"}],"correctOptionId":"q14a","explanation":"Cacao beans were currency. A tomato cost about one bean, a turkey about 100, a canoe about 8,000, and the annual tribute to Tenochtitlan ran to about 24 million beans.","citation":"Lesson: Food of the Gods"},{"id":"q15","questionText":"What does the Nahuatl word xocolatl, the root of chocolate, mean?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q15a","text":"Bitter water"},{"id":"q15b","text":"Sweet milk"},{"id":"q15c","text":"Food of the gods"},{"id":"q15d","text":"Golden drink"}],"correctOptionId":"q15a","explanation":"Xocolatl means bitter water, describing the original unsweetened Mesoamerican drink. Theobroma, a Greek word, is the one that means food of the gods.","citation":"Lesson: Cacao, Cocoa, Chocolate"},{"id":"q16","questionText":"What happened to chocolate after cacao reached Spain in 1528?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q16a","text":"Sugar was added, turning a bitter drink into a sweet luxury"},{"id":"q16b","text":"It was banned in Europe"},{"id":"q16c","text":"It stayed exactly as the Aztec drank it"},{"id":"q16d","text":"It was used only as medicine and never as a drink"}],"correctOptionId":"q16a","explanation":"In Spain, sugar was added to xocolatl, transforming the bitter ceremonial drink into the sweet luxury Europe came to love. The sweet bar is a European invention.","citation":"Lesson: Conquest and the Great Reversal"},{"id":"q17","questionText":"What was the great reversal in who captured cacao's value after colonization?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q17a","text":"Locally captured value fell from 100 percent to about 3 to 6 percent"},{"id":"q17b","text":"Local value rose to 100 percent"},{"id":"q17c","text":"Nothing changed"},{"id":"q17d","text":"Farmers began keeping all the profit"}],"correctOptionId":"q17a","explanation":"Before colonization, indigenous communities captured 100 percent of cacao's value. After, growers captured only about 3 to 6 percent, while European processors took the rest.","citation":"Lesson: Conquest and the Great Reversal"},{"id":"q18","questionText":"What did the 2020 NORC study document about West African cacao?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q18a","text":"About 1.56 million children working in cacao production"},{"id":"q18b","text":"That child labor had ended"},{"id":"q18c","text":"That cacao farming had stopped"},{"id":"q18d","text":"That all farmers earned a living wage"}],"correctOptionId":"q18a","explanation":"The 2020 NORC study found about 1.56 million children in cacao labor in West Africa, with most exposed to hazardous conditions and most families below the poverty line.","citation":"Lesson: The Children in the Cacao"},{"id":"q19","questionText":"About how large is the global chocolate market?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q19a","text":"About 161.5 billion dollars a year"},{"id":"q19b","text":"About 1.6 billion dollars a year"},{"id":"q19c","text":"About 16 billion dollars a year"},{"id":"q19d","text":"About 2 trillion dollars a year"}],"correctOptionId":"q19a","explanation":"The global chocolate market is about 161.5 billion dollars a year, which makes the farmers' tiny share of it all the more striking.","citation":"Lesson: The Commodity Trap"},{"id":"q20","questionText":"In a conventional 3 dollar chocolate bar, about what share reaches the cacao farmer?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q20a","text":"About 3 to 6 percent"},{"id":"q20b","text":"About 35 to 40 percent"},{"id":"q20c","text":"About 70 percent"},{"id":"q20d","text":"About 90 percent"}],"correctOptionId":"q20a","explanation":"The farmer receives only about 3 to 6 percent of a conventional bar. Manufacturing takes about 35 to 40 percent and marketing and retail about 30 to 35 percent.","citation":"Lesson: The Commodity Trap"},{"id":"q21","questionText":"What is a monopsony, and how does it show up in chocolate?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q21a","text":"A market with few buyers controlling many sellers; about 4 companies control 60 percent of processing"},{"id":"q21b","text":"A market with one seller and many buyers"},{"id":"q21c","text":"A market with no buyers"},{"id":"q21d","text":"A government tax on chocolate"}],"correctOptionId":"q21a","explanation":"A monopsony has few buyers and many sellers. In cacao, roughly 4 companies control about 60 percent of processing, which keeps prices to farmers low.","citation":"Lesson: The Commodity Trap"},{"id":"q22","questionText":"What is the commodity trap?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q22a","text":"When raw-material producers stay poor while processors capture most of the value"},{"id":"q22b","text":"When a crop becomes too easy to grow"},{"id":"q22c","text":"When farmers earn the most money"},{"id":"q22d","text":"When a product is banned"}],"correctOptionId":"q22a","explanation":"The commodity trap is when producers of a raw material receive minimal value while the people who process and sell it capture most of the profit, as cacao farmers do.","citation":"Lesson: The Commodity Trap"},{"id":"q23","questionText":"How much would a 3 dollar chocolate bar cost if farmers were paid a living wage?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q23a","text":"About 3.45 dollars, roughly a 15 percent increase"},{"id":"q23b","text":"About 10 dollars, a tripling"},{"id":"q23c","text":"About 30 dollars"},{"id":"q23d","text":"There would be no change"}],"correctOptionId":"q23a","explanation":"Paying farmers a living wage would raise a 3 dollar bar to about 3.45 dollars, only about a 15 percent increase. The gap is small for the buyer and large for the farmer.","citation":"Lesson: The Fifty-Cent Question"},{"id":"q24","questionText":"How does fair trade compare with conventional cacao on child labor?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q24a","text":"Child labor prevalence is lower under fair trade, about 12 percent versus about 31 percent conventional"},{"id":"q24b","text":"Fair trade has more child labor"},{"id":"q24c","text":"They are exactly the same"},{"id":"q24d","text":"Neither has any child labor"}],"correctOptionId":"q24a","explanation":"Fair trade cacao shows lower child-labor prevalence, about 12 percent versus about 31 percent for conventional, and higher school attendance. It is not perfect, but the gap is real.","citation":"Lesson: The Fifty-Cent Question"},{"id":"q25","questionText":"The English word 'cocoa' came about how?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q25a","text":"As an English corruption of cacao, likely a shipping-document misspelling"},{"id":"q25b","text":"From a Greek word for sweet"},{"id":"q25c","text":"From the name of a Spanish city"},{"id":"q25d","text":"From a brand name invented in 1900"}],"correctOptionId":"q25a","explanation":"Cocoa is an English corruption of cacao, probably a shipping-document misspelling. The word trail from cacahuatl to cacao to cocoa mirrors the shift from indigenous control to European appropriation.","citation":"Lesson: Cacao, Cocoa, Chocolate"},{"id":"q26","questionText":"In the conquest, how did Cortes describe xocolatl in his letters?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q26a","text":"As a drink more for pigs than for men, while still claiming its economic value"},{"id":"q26b","text":"As the finest drink he had ever tasted"},{"id":"q26c","text":"As a sacred Spanish tradition"},{"id":"q26d","text":"As poison"}],"correctOptionId":"q26a","explanation":"Cortes called xocolatl a drink more for pigs than for men, dismissing its cultural value while claiming its economic value. Set against the Maya 'food of the gods,' it is a sharp lesson in perspective and bias.","citation":"Lesson: Two Ways of Seeing a Bean"},{"id":"q27","questionText":"Why is 'Belgian chocolate' or 'Swiss chocolate' a case of geographic misdirection?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q27a","text":"Belgium and Switzerland grow zero cacao; the beans come from the tropics"},{"id":"q27b","text":"Belgium grows most of the world's cacao"},{"id":"q27c","text":"Switzerland is in the cacao belt"},{"id":"q27d","text":"The terms refer to where cacao is grown"}],"correctOptionId":"q27a","explanation":"Belgium and Switzerland grow no cacao at all. The labels point to where chocolate is processed and branded, not where the beans are grown, which hides the tropical farmers.","citation":"Lesson: Reading a Chocolate Ad"},{"id":"q28","questionText":"Which conditions does cacao need that make it so place-bound?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q28a","text":"Hot, wet, shaded, frost-free, low-elevation forest near the equator"},{"id":"q28b","text":"Cold, dry mountain air"},{"id":"q28c","text":"Salty coastal soil and full sun"},{"id":"q28d","text":"Desert heat with irrigation"}],"correctOptionId":"q28a","explanation":"Cacao needs a hot, wet, shaded, frost-free, low-elevation forest near the equator, plus forest midges to pollinate it. There are not many such places, which is why the belt is so narrow.","citation":"Lesson: The Cacao Tree and the Narrowest Belt"},{"id":"q29","questionText":"Where do the first written references to cacao appear, around 300 CE?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q29a","text":"In Maya hieroglyphs at Copan"},{"id":"q29b","text":"In Spanish ledgers"},{"id":"q29c","text":"In Greek philosophy"},{"id":"q29d","text":"In West African records"}],"correctOptionId":"q29a","explanation":"Maya hieroglyphs at Copan, around 300 CE, include some of the first written references to cacao, part of the peak of indigenous cacao culture.","citation":"Lesson: Food of the Gods"},{"id":"q30","questionText":"When did colonial powers shift cacao production to West Africa?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q30a","text":"In the early 1900s"},{"id":"q30b","text":"Before 1500"},{"id":"q30c","text":"In the 1990s"},{"id":"q30d","text":"Cacao has always grown only in West Africa"}],"correctOptionId":"q30a","explanation":"Colonial powers established cacao plantations in West Africa in the early 1900s, shifting production from cacao's American homeland to Africa, where most of it grows today.","citation":"Lesson: Conquest and the Great Reversal"},{"id":"q31","questionText":"When did fair trade cacao certification begin?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q31a","text":"1988"},{"id":"q31b","text":"1519"},{"id":"q31c","text":"300 CE"},{"id":"q31d","text":"2020"}],"correctOptionId":"q31a","explanation":"Fair trade cacao certification began in 1988, as part of the response to plantation labor concerns. 1519 was the conquest, 300 CE the Copan hieroglyphs, and 2020 the NORC labor study.","citation":"Lesson: The Children in the Cacao"},{"id":"q32","questionText":"What does value-added processing look like for cacao?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q32a","text":"Raw cacao worth about 2,400 dollars a tonne becomes chocolate products worth far more at retail"},{"id":"q32b","text":"Raw cacao is worth more than the finished chocolate"},{"id":"q32c","text":"Processing lowers the value"},{"id":"q32d","text":"Cacao cannot be processed"}],"correctOptionId":"q32a","explanation":"Value-added processing transforms raw cacao, worth about 2,400 dollars a tonne, into chocolate products worth far more at retail. Most of that added value is captured outside the growing countries.","citation":"Lesson: The Commodity Trap"},{"id":"q33","questionText":"Moctezuma II is said to have consumed how much cacao?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q33a","text":"About 50 or more cups of the cacao drink a day"},{"id":"q33b","text":"One cup a year"},{"id":"q33c","text":"None; it was forbidden to him"},{"id":"q33d","text":"Only at his coronation"}],"correctOptionId":"q33a","explanation":"Moctezuma II reportedly drank 50 or more cups of the cacao drink a day, a sign of imperial excess.
